posts.name: Lee Braem
posts.cooked:
I just received an offer from Medlock Ames for purchasing their olive oil. I’ve received other such offers over the years but have only bought some bottles when I am actually in wine country. I searched the forum and only saw something relevant for oil sold by the Rare Wine Co. Finally, this seems the best board forum to post this question.

Does anyone have opinions or thoughts about which wineries produce the best olive oil? Whether it’s buying wine, olive oil, or other bling, I’d like to support the wineries. Thanks.

posts.name: James Slawney
posts.cooked: Terroir al Limit Priorat Caragols Olive Oil Extra Virgin (less than 2000 bottles produced, 20 sitting here with me). Sorry, it is hard to source in NJ. Maybe impossible.

posts.name: CJ Beazley
posts.cooked: A few years ago a neighbor I had helped sent a gift package from Round Pond that had wine, olive oil and vinegar they were all very good.

posts.name: Paul Miller
posts.cooked:
Interested to hear what others say. I’ve been to several wineries that also offer olive oil at their tasting room, but I never thought about taking notes. Sometimes they’ve seemed light, and other times astringent, or sometimes too grassy. We visited Turley earlier this year, and bought a bottle from them; however, we gave it away as a gift. I remember it struck us as a good tasting olive oil.

Years ago we visited a small winery in Napa, and they made olive oil. I was fascinated by the idea at the time. The only reason we learned that they did so was because the owner walked in with a few bottles. We, are someone else, asked if there were samples or tastes. The owner made some comment like, “It so limited there is no f*cking way I’m going to let people sample it!”

posts.name: Mar.kus Meer
posts.cooked: Marquiliani is great for finishing. I used Occhipinti for bulk use. Heard good things about Vieux Telegraphed but never tried.

posts.name: Bill Tex Landreth
posts.cooked: Araujo

posts.name: A. So
posts.cooked: Valentini

DaVero Farms and Winery (Healdsburg) might be one of the few locations where Olive Oil production is prioritized on the same level as their wine-making. They have a large grove of Italian varietals propagated from cuttings they brought in from Italy. Their Olio Nuovo truly is green gold. And, their oil has been praised publicly by a few celebrity chefs - including a formerly popular food network famed Italian chef.

posts.name: Kyle Chen
posts.cooked:
If you like Tuscan style olive oil made by California wineries, by far the best is DaVero in Dry Creek or Hudson Ranch in Carneros. For both, the wines are pretty mediocre, but the oils are excellent.

Otherwise, in my experience, most higher end wineries that make olive oils in California are Mission blend derivatives which are quite boring IMO.

posts.name: S teve R edenbaugh
posts.cooked: I have always loved the olive oil produced by Bruce Cohn and bearing the B R Cohn label. I know the oil has garnered great press and many awards over the years.

posts.name: Andy Steinman
posts.cooked: Durant here in Oregon

posts.name: Nathan Smyth
posts.cooked:
A few years ago a neighbor I had helped sent a gift package from Round Pond that had wine, olive oil and vinegar they were all very good.

I have never personally been to Round Pond, but I have relatives who have been there, and they were very impressed.

Well… I particularly like the olive oil of TablasCreek. It has that peppery character I like in young olive oils. But it sells out pretty fast.
Tom

posts.name: NED VALOIS
posts.cooked: Try Rare Wine !

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: The Massican olive oil was very good, but I vastly prefer Berserker Nola Palomar’s Veleta Olive Oil over anything I have had from a winery. Heck I prefer it over just about anything!

posts.name: Ryan A.ns.el
posts.cooked:
Araujo

+1 (now Eisele Vineyards)

Also Abreu

Looks like Bella Oaks is releasing one this year. Never tried it but look forward to if given the chance.

This!

posts.name: Andrew Demaree
posts.cooked: Cimarossa produces an excellent olive oil up on Howell Mtn from five Italian varieties. Small production, though, and it tends to sell out.

posts.name: Jonathan Jaggers
posts.cooked: Both Long Meadow Ranch and Quivira make very good olive oil

posts.name: Paul Huff
posts.cooked: You should consider Veleta Olive Oil from our very own Berserker Nola Palomar…imported directly from her farm in Spain. I buy a case of 12-bottles every BerserkerDay!

posts.name: Brig Campbell
posts.cooked:
Hello, our own Nola’s Veleta is amazing. We don’t cook with it, too good to waste that way. It gets drizzled on caprese, gourmet pizza, salad dressing, etc.

Berserker secret.

GOURMETS OF SPAIN / GOURMET SPANISH DELICACIES

Veleta Extra Virgin Olive Oil

Veleta Extra Virgin Olive Oil, EVOO, Granada, Alpujarra, Mediterranean Diet, Heart Healthy diet, Tapas, Spain Spanish Olive Oil

posts.name: Paul Miller
posts.cooked: Yeah. Nola’s is great. As is her balsamic.

posts.name: Pat_Martin
posts.cooked: The best olive oil-wine combo I’ve had is from Selvapiana in Chianti. Fontodi is a close second.

posts.name: Travis A L L E N
posts.cooked: Vieux Telegraph is great, also love il Caberlot and clos mogador olive oil.

posts.name: Tom DeBiase
posts.cooked:
Have had many of the above, none as good as Preston Olive Oil from Preston Winery in Dry Creek Valley.

store.prestonvineyards.com

Preston Farm and Winery

A family-operated farm and winery in Dry Creek Valley, also producing olive oil and hearth breads.

posts.name: Doug Schulman
posts.cooked: I have no idea what’s the best, but the Paolo Bea oils are great.

posts.name: Christopher Foley
posts.cooked: Count me in the Veleta camp. My wife and I will consume about 18 (500ml) bottles this year. I add a little to sautee vegetables, mushrooms, eggs in a non stick pan, but the vast majority goes into salad dressing and pesto. We also like to occasionally pour some in a bowl, add salt, pepper and herbs, and dip bread, as an appetizer before dinner. Nola’s oil is so fruity and distinct. I love it.
